# Questions in Livestream 54

Live Stream #54: Amun-Re Dumps Amonet.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:43</a> | How should we understand the reports of "armies in the sky" in Josephus, Tacitus, and related sources? | He treated them as a portent image that fits biblical locust-and-army symbolism rather than as straightforward proof of a christophany. | He connected the reports to Joel's imagery, where locusts are described like horses, chariots, and the Lord's army. On that reading, the Jerusalem accounts are best understood as an ominous sign around the Roman siege: a swarm or army-like phenomenon in the sky interpreted through biblical prophetic symbolism as impending judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:32</a> | When was the Torah written: Bronze Age or Iron Age? | He argued that the source documents go back to the Bronze Age, while editing and compilation belong to the Iron Age. | He distinguished between the age of the source texts and the later process of stitching them together. He argued that Bronze Age Egyptian vocabulary in the Torah and the prophets' apparent knowledge of Torah point to older source documents, while phrases such as references to there being no king in Israel suggest later editorial glosses during the monarchy or later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=1012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:52</a> | Do you know Christian historians who are experts on Rome and first-century Judea? | He recommended Craig Evans, William H. C. Frend, and John McRay as useful starting points. | He named Craig Evans for work on Jesus and archaeology, William H. C. Frend for early Christian archaeology, and John McRay for archaeology and the New Testament. He framed them as starting points for the archaeology of the early Christian world and late Judaism, while noting that broader classical archaeology has many more specialists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=1164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:24</a> | Should someone ditch Hebrew and Greek grammar books and just use Strong's with an interlinear? | No. He strongly preferred real grammars and modern lexicons, saying Strong's lacks nuance and ignores language development. | He answered emphatically that Strong's and interlinears should not replace grammar and real lexicons. His main criticism was that Strong's is outdated, lacks nuance, and treats words synchronically, as though meanings stay the same across all periods of Hebrew and Greek, which can produce serious mistakes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=1451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:11</a> | Other than Dune, what is your favorite science-fiction book? | He said he does not have one favorite book and tends to follow authors more than individual titles or series. | He explained that he tends to remember and follow authors rather than pick one favorite book. He mentioned a long personal history with science-fiction conventions and authors, including Clifford Simak, Robert Silverberg, Poul Anderson, Kevin J. Anderson, David Weber, Esther Friesner, Melanie Rawn, and Frederick Pohl. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=1736" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:56</a> | In Daniel 3:25, is the better translation "god" or "gods," and should the figure be read as a christophany? | He said the wording is plural, "like a son of the gods," and the text allows a divine, christophanic, theophanic, or angelic reading without enough evidence to insist on one. | Working from the Masoretic text, he said the form is a regular plural rather than a majestic singular, so "like a son of the gods" is the best translation. Because it is a visual comparison, he said it does not settle whether the figure is a christophany, theophany, or angelic manifestation; the text shows divine action but does not identify the manifestation precisely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=2111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:11</a> | How old was Isaac at the Aqedah, and when should biblical ages and numbers be read literally or idiomatically? | He leaned toward Isaac being a young teenager and said numbers must be judged by context rather than one rigid rule. | He said Scripture does not give Isaac's exact age, but the narrative makes better sense if Isaac was young enough for Abraham to bind him, probably an early teenager rather than an adult. On numbers, he preferred "idiomatic" over "symbolic," arguing that Hebrew, like modern languages, uses numbers flexibly; common numbers such as 40, 12, 7, 80, 110, and 120 can carry contextual meaning while still following a logic within the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=2633" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:53</a> | Does each person of the Trinity having a will conflict with the oneness of God? | No. He said the persons are unified in divine essence and purpose, so distinct wills do not imply sinful conflict. | He rejected the claim that distinct personal wills necessarily create division, because the divine persons share one essence and are unified in purpose. Conflict would require ungodly traits such as selfishness, rebellion, or divisiveness, whereas a Trinitarian account can even explain divine goodness as the persons being good to one another before creation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=2961" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:21</a> | Can viewers post questions before the livestream begins? | Yes. He encouraged pre-posting questions in the live chat once the livestream thumbnail is up, especially for complex topics. | He said the livestream thumbnail usually goes up on Tuesday or Wednesday, and once it does, the live chat is open for questions and super chats. He checks those posts, saves them so they do not drop off, and especially encourages early posting for complex questions that need preparation, such as text-critical questions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=3239" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:59</a> | If you were making art, how would you depict angels? | He said it depends on the medium and the effect being aimed at, such as power, protection, or historical symbolism. | He said there is no single right depiction because the answer depends on the purpose and medium of the artwork. He contrasted Late Bronze Age winged figures, Second Temple images with multiple faces and wings, and later European men-with-wings imagery, then said he would choose differently for power, protection, historical accuracy, sculpture, mural, digital art, or a 3D model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=3630" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:30</a> | Are the Nag Hammadi texts representative of average Gnostic Christian belief, and are Gnostic ideas resurging? | He said Gnostic ideas never fully disappeared and that Nag Hammadi is a particular cache from a monastic setting, not a full cross-section of ordinary Gnostic belief. | He described Gnosticism as adaptable, allegorical, and marked by deprecation of the flesh, aeons or multiple divine beings, and saving secret knowledge. He said Nag Hammadi was likely a cache of forbidden texts brought into and hidden around a monastic setting, so it represents Christian Gnostic sectarian material rather than ordinary Christian belief; he also said Gnostic ideas have continued in various forms and periodically regain appeal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=3997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:37</a> | Who are the angels in 2 Peter 2:4-7: watchers or other demonic beings? | He distinguished watchers from demons, saying 2 Peter is about fallen angels and that watchers are a different class of fallen spiritual beings. | Reading 2 Peter's reference to angels cast into Tartarus, he said the passage concerns angels who sinned, not watchers in the stricter Second Temple sense. He treated watchers as a higher or different class akin to principalities and powers, while demons are fallen angels; the point of the passage is that if God did not spare higher fallen beings, he will not spare unrighteous humans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=4445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:05</a> | What are Messianic Jewish congregations like, including concerns about doctrine and lost-tribes ideas? | He described the movement as a broad tent that is still sorting out its identity, warned that some branches go in questionable directions, and praised New Testament-shaped Messianic Jewish congregations. | Drawing on his own work with a Messianic Jewish congregation, he said the movement is diverse and still wrestling with how Jewish identity relates to faith in Jesus. He warned that some branches move toward legalism or questionable lost-tribes emphases, but he praised congregations that live culturally as Jews while grounding doctrine in New Testament freedom in Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=4863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:03</a> | How would he steelman young-earth creationism against archaeological sites older than 6,000 years, such as Gobekli Tepe? | He said a strict 6,000-year chronology is hard to maintain archaeologically and that a stronger young-earth case would have to rely more on exegesis and scientific arguments than simplistic timeline harmonization. | He distinguished American young-earth creationism, which often doubles down on a 6,000-year chronology from genealogies, from broader or continental versions that allow a much older but still young earth. To steelman the position, he would abandon the strict 6,000-year reading, use better exegesis, and lean on scientific arguments such as genetic or isotopic models, perhaps in a 50,000 to 150,000-year range. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=5242" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:22</a> | Do you know good books on the Hittites and Neo-Assyrians? | He recommended Harry Hoffner and Trevor Bryce for Hittite studies and mentioned Alan Millard, Grayson, and Lambert as useful Assyrian-related starting points. | For the Hittites he recommended Harry Hoffner and Trevor Bryce, especially Bryce's work on the Hittite kingdom, and noted Luwian-language studies as a related path. For Neo-Assyria he said good general overviews are harder because the field is so technical, but he pointed to Alan Millard, Grayson on chronicles and royal inscriptions, and Lambert as useful places to begin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=5555" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:35</a> | Is there more than one Satan in the Bible? | He said "satan" can function as a role or title for accusers, while there is still one preeminent Satan; Job, the New Testament, and Peter's rebuke illustrate the range. | He said Job presents "the satan" as an accuser or prosecutorial figure in the divine council, while the New Testament expands the figure to the serpent and chief enemy. The term can also be applied functionally to anyone acting as an accuser, as when Jesus rebukes Peter; so there can be many "satans" in a role sense while still one preeminent Satan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=6143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:23</a> | Have you considered a collaboration with World History by a Jew? | He said collaborations are possible when the fit is good, but his time is limited and he does not know that channel well. | He said he does not know much about that channel but is open to collaborations when there is a good conversational fit. Because he does YouTube part time and has many requests, he normally limits collaborations to about one per week and looks for partners who can understand and engage the arguments in a way that benefits the audience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=6444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:24</a> | Who or what was Azazel in Leviticus 16, and did that idea change by the Second Temple period? | He said the Leviticus context points first to the loss or removal of sin rather than a named evil being, though later interpretations personified or conflated Azazel more strongly. | He read Azazel in Leviticus 16 as tied to the complete removal or loss of sin: one goat covers sin sacrificially and the other carries the people's sins away into the wilderness so they are not found again. He said Second Temple literature later conflated Azazel with goat-demon ideas, but that this is a later distortion rather than the best reading of Leviticus in its own context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=6850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:10</a> | Was Jesus born during Sukkot around 4 BCE? | He said it is difficult to know exactly and would not divide over it, though he personally leaned more toward a spring birth and thought 4 BCE was plausible. | He said a Sukkot birth is possible but conjectural, with no decisive reason either to affirm or reject it. He was more willing to say that 4 BCE is a strong candidate for Jesus' birth, perhaps with a range as early as 6 BCE, because of chronological considerations involving Augustus, censuses, and the known error in Anno Domini dating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=7125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:45</a> | How can someone tell whether a Bible translation is good? | He said the best check is knowledge of the original languages, but otherwise readers must rely on qualified evaluators who know both the source and target language well. | He said the most direct way to evaluate a translation is to know Hebrew and Greek, because then a reader can verify it independently. Without that, especially in a non-English language, the reader must depend on trustworthy scholars who know both the original languages and the target language, while also considering text base, translation method, and whether the available translation serves the reader's spiritual needs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=7430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:50</a> | Does the Bible teach matrilineal descent, and is Ezra's expulsion of foreign wives evidence for it? | He said Ezra is about covenant contamination through foreign gods, while matrilineal descent becomes much clearer only in the later rabbinic period. | He treated Ezra's foreign-wives issue as a covenant and worship problem: men who married foreign women were also being drawn into foreign gods, so the return from exile was framed as rebuilding a Torah-based society. He said biblical genealogies are generally patrilineal, while matrilineal descent becomes dominant only later in the rabbinic period, especially after diaspora conditions complicated paternal descent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=7817" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:17</a> | Should scholars abandon "Gnostic" as a heuristic label? | No. He said the category still has technical value because it names a real cluster of recurring beliefs even if the groups were diverse. | He argued that "Gnostic" remains useful because recurring ideas such as trapped souls, bad flesh, good spirit, saving secret knowledge, and allegorical reinterpretation appear across the movement. He compared it to "New Age" as an umbrella term: not every group shares every detail, but the cluster is coherent enough for scholarly shorthand, and abandoning all such jargon makes discussion cumbersome. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=8324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:44</a> | What is your view of abstract objects and essences, such as Platonic, Aristotelian, or nominalist views? | He said he does not take a strong position and would need someone more specialized in philosophy for a fuller answer. | He said he had studied Plato and some Aristotle but does not hold a settled position on abstract objects, essences, forms, or nominalism. He found Platonic and Aristotelian approaches interesting but difficult to prove, admitted he did not know Ockham's view well enough, and deferred fuller treatment to someone more conversant in philosophy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=8503" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:43</a> | Before Christ, was Yahweh semi-deist, revealing himself only rarely except to Moses and a few prophets? | He rejected "semi-deist" as the right label, saying Yahweh is transcendent but still makes himself known through Israel, other prophets, and creation. | He said Yahweh is not simply absent from the world, because he reveals himself to Israel, to non-Israelite figures such as Balaam, possibly through peoples such as Midianites or Shasu, and through creation as in Romans 1-2. The better category is transcendence: God withdraws from sin to avoid immediate judgment, but he still remains involved and self-revealing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=8850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:30</a> | Is Tartarus basically synonymous with Gehenna? | He said the two images are not identical in origin, but in biblical context both point to the worst place of judgment. | He distinguished their backgrounds: Gehenna is tied to the Valley of Hinnom and the Topheth, while Tartarus comes from Greek imagery for the worst place of punishment. In biblical usage, however, he said both functionally point toward the place for the worst rebellion and judgment, connected with the lake of fire and punishment prepared for rebellious spiritual beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=9050" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:50</a> | How did Sabbath and Passover obligations apply to foreign slaves in Israel? | He stressed that everyone in the household, including slaves, was bound by Sabbath rest, while Passover participation was regulated by circumcision. | He limited the answer to Sabbath and Passover because the larger slavery question was too broad for the stream. For Sabbath, he cited Exodus 20 to say everyone in the household rests: family, servants, slaves, cattle, and sojourners. For Passover, he cited Exodus 12 to say no uncircumcised person may eat it, but a purchased slave or sojourner may participate if circumcised. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=9612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:12</a> | Why was the nation of Israel chosen? | He answered from Deuteronomy: Israel was chosen because God loved the patriarchs and intended to work through their descendants. | He cited Deuteronomy 4:37 to say God chose Israel because he loved Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, not because Israel was inherently superior. He then connected this to God's broader character of love and covenant patience, emphasizing that divine loving-kindness extends far beyond wrath and that Israel's election is rooted in God's love for the patriarchs and their descendants. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FT2gTO3om8U?t=10007" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:47</a> | If the Torah was edited, what does that mean for biblical inerrancy? | He said it depends on what kind of editing is meant: content-changing redaction is a problem, but compilation, glosses, spelling updates, and inspired additions need not threaten inerrancy. | He distinguished harmful content-changing redaction from editing that compiles existing Mosaic materials, adds glosses, updates spelling, or appends inspired material such as the death of Moses. In his view, inerrancy is threatened if later editors substantially change the text's content, but not by respectful compilation or inspired additions that preserve the message. |
